questions.text: Negative feedback mechanism is the characteristic of which class?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: While fish do not have regulatory mechanisms to maintain homeostasis, the negative feedback mechanism is not exclusive to Class fish.
Amphibians also have regulatory mechanisms, but again, the negative feedback mechanism is not specific to Class Amphibia
Reptilia, like fish and amphibians, have regulatory mechanisms to maintain homeostasis, tbut he negative feedback mechanism is not a defining characteristic of Class Reptilia
The negative feedback mechanism is a regulatory process commonly found in mammals.

questions.explanation: Negative feedback is a type of regulation in biological systems in which the end product of a process, in turn, reduces the stimulus of that same process. Feedback, in general, is a regulatory mechanism present in many biological reactions. By allowing certain pathways to be turned off and on, the body can control various aspects of its internal environment. Examples of negative feedback include regulation of blood glucose levels and temperature regulation of the body.

options.explanation: The function of papillary muscles is to prevent the backward flow or regurgitation of blood from the ventricles into the atria

options.text: Push the blood from right atrium to left atrium
options.explanation: Pressure due to continuous blood entry into the heart and opening and closing of valves pushes blood from right atrium to left atrium and from left atrium to aorta.

options.explanation: The papillary muscles are not responsible for pushing blood from the left atrium to the aorta

questions.explanation: The papillary muscles are muscles located in the ventricles of the heart. They attach to the cusps of the atrioventricular valves (also known as the mitral and tricuspid valves) via the chordae tendineae and contract to prevent inversion or prolapse of these valves on systole (or ventricular contraction).

options.explanation: The pancreas contains exocrine glands that produce enzymes important to digestion. These enzymes include trypsin and chymotrypsin to digest proteins; amylase for the digestion of carbohydrates, and lipase to break down fats.

The liver secretes no digestive enzymes. However, it secretes a yellow juice called bile which helps in digestion in the small intestine. In addition, the liver regulates blood sugar level, detoxifies the blood, and breaks down excess amino acids.

The stomach contains many types of cells which perform their own functions. The surface mucous cells secrete a layer of mucus that protects the stomach lining. questions.text: Gaseous exchange in plants takes place through the:

options.explanation: Stomata regulate gas exchange between the plant and the environment and control water loss by changing the size of the stomatal pore. This stomatal movement is affected by several environmental stimuli, such as relative humidity, CO2 concentration, and light intensity.

The primary and absolute most important role of mesophyll is its role in photosynthesis. Photosynthesis, as you may know, is the process by which a plant takes carbon dioxide, water, and sunlight and creates sugars, which result in the plant having energy.
The endoderm is the innermost germ layer that forms the linings of the respiratory and gastrointestinal tracts, and their associated organs, during embryonic development.

Xylem is the specialized tissue of vascular plants that transports water and nutrients from the plant–soil interface to stems and leaves and provides mechanical support and storage. The water-conducting function of the xylem is one of the major distinguishing features of vascular plants.

questions.explanation: The exchange of oxygen and carbon dioxide in the leaf (as well as the loss of water vapor in transpiration) occurs through pores called 'stomata' (singular = stoma). Normally, stomata open when light strikes the leaf in the morning and close during the night.

questions.text: Digestion of which food components starts from the oral cavity?

options.explanation: Protein is a vital nutrient for almost every part of your body. It's digested in the stomach and small intestine. Most of the protein digestion occurs in the small intestine.

Fat digestion begins in the stomach. Some of the byproducts of fat digestion can be directly absorbed in the stomach. When the fat enters the small intestine, the gallbladder and pancreas secrete substances to further break down the fat.

Digestion of carbohydrates (starches into glucose molecules) starts in the mouth but primarily takes place in the small intestine by the action of specific enzymes secreted from the pancreas (e.g. α-amylase and α-glucosidase).

Essentially all vitamin absorption occurs in the small intestine. Stomach juices, pancreatic secretions, bile from the liver, and the integrity of the wall of the intestine — all have some influence on how much of the vitamin is absorbed.

options.text: Conversion of glucose to glycogen in liver cells
options.explanation: Conversion of glucose to glycogen decreases blood glucose levels. It is a function of insulin.
A decrease in blood glucose level is done by insulin.

options.text: Increased uptake of glucose by muscle cells
options.explanation: Muscle cells cause glucose to be used while doing work. It is not a function of glucagon because glucagon causes an increase in the blood glucose level.

options.text: Production of cyclic AMP in target cells
options.explanation: 'Glucagon' has an opposite role to 'insulin' i.e. it acts to release blood glucose in the plasma when blood has a low level of glucose in it. When glucagon is released, it stimulates the liver to break down glycogen into glucose. The chief role of cyclic AMP in several tissues is to promote the mobilization of glucose and fatty acid reserves. In the liver, glucagon, and catecholamines cause an increase in the intracellular level of cyclic AMP by stimulating adenylyl cyclase.

questions.explanation: 'Glucagon' has the opposite role to 'insulin' i.e. it mobilizes blood glucose in the plasma when you’re hypoglycemic. When glucagon is released, it can perform the following tasks: Stimulating the liver to break down glycogen to be released into the blood as glucose. Activating gluconeogenesis is the conversion of amino acids into glucose. Breaking down stored fat (triglycerides) into fatty acids for use as fuel by cells. All of the other options, except D, explain the role of insulin and not glucagon. By simply using an elimination method, we can deduce that D is the right answer. Just a piece of additional information; The chief role of cyclic AMP in several tissues seems to be to facilitate or promote the mobilization of glucose and fatty acid reserves. In the liver, glucagon, and catecholamines cause an increase in the intracellular level of cyclic AMP by stimulating adenylyl cyclase.

questions.text: Which organ is called the body thermostat?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The pituitary gland is referred to as the “master gland” because it monitors and regulates many bodily functions through the hormones that it produces, including growth and sexual development and function.

The kidneys act like a filter to remove wastes and extra fluid from your body. Your kidneys filter blood each day to make about 1 to 2 quarts of urine. The urine contains wastes and extra fluid. This prevents the buildup of wastes and fluid to keep your body healthy.

The hypothalamus functions as a type of thermostat for the body. It sets a desired body temperature and stimulates either heat production and retention to raise the blood temperature to a higher setting or sweating and vasodilation to cool the blood to a lower temperature.

Adrenal glands produce hormones that help regulate your metabolism, immune system, blood pressure, response to stress, and other essential functions. Adrenal glands are composed of two parts — the cortex and the medulla — which are each responsible for producing different hormones.

questions.explanation: Much like a thermostat regulates the temperature inside your home, the hypothalamus regulates your body temperature, responding to internal and external stimuli and making adjustments to keep the body within one or two degrees of 98.6 degrees.

questions.text: Which of the following substances helps to concentrate urine?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Aldosterone is a steroid hormone made by the adrenal cortex (the outer layer of the adrenal gland). It helps control the balance of water and salts in the kidney by keeping sodium in and releasing potassium from the body.

ADH is a hormone that helps blood vessels constrict and helps the kidneys control the amount of water and salt in the body. This helps control blood pressure and the amount of urine that is made. It is responsible for the concentration of urine.
Glucosterone or glucocorticoid is a hormone that helps blood vessels constrict and helps the kidneys control the amount of water and salt in the body. This helps control blood pressure.

Thyroxine is a hormone produced by the thyroid gland which is responsible for secreting it into the bloodstream. Once in the bloodstream, thyroxine travels to the organs, like the liver and kidneys, where it is converted to its active form of triiodothyronine.

questions.explanation: Antidiuretic hormone (ADH)is a chemical produced in the brain that causes the kidneys to release less water, decreasing the amount of urine produced. If less urine is produced, then the content of the urine becomes more concentrated.

questions.text: The multinucleated mass of the bone-breaking cells is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Osteoclasts are the cells that degrade bone to initiate normal bone remodeling and mediate bone loss in pathologic conditions by increasing their resorptive activity. They are, in fact, a multinucleated mass of cells.

Osteoblasts are the bone cells that are primarily responsible for synthesizing bone matrix proteins and minerals during early bone formation in the embryo but also control bone formation and mineralization throughout life.

Osteogenesis is a congenital bone disorder. It is also known as brittle bone disease. A child born with osteogenesis may have soft bones that break (fracture) easily, bones that are not formed normally, and other problems. Signs and symptoms may range from mild to severe.
Osteocytes are the longest-living bone cell, making up 90–95% of cells in bone tissue. Osteocytes regulate local mineral deposition and chemistry at the bone matrix level.

questions.explanation: 'Osteoclasts' are multinucleated cells that derive from hematopoietic progenitors in the bone marrow, which also give rise to monocytes in peripheral blood. Osteoclasts break down bone tissue, and along with that, osteoblasts and osteocytes form the structural components of bone.

questions.text: Chief material present in the cell wall of plants, fungal, and prokaryotic cells is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Proteins are essential constituents of plant cell walls; they are involved in modifications of cell wall components, wall structure, signaling, and interactions with plasma membrane proteins at the cell surface.
Lipids are found primarily in bacterial cell walls. They maintain a selective permeability for the import and export of water-soluble compounds in the bacteria.

The polysaccharide found in plants including hemicellulose, pectin, and cellulose. In fungi, chitin is found which is also a polysaccharide. Bacterial cell walls consist mainly of the polysaccharide i.e., peptidoglycan.

Phospholipids are major membrane lipids that consist of lipid bilayers. This basic cellular structure acts as a barrier to protect the cell against various environmental insults and more importantly, enables multiple cellular processes to occur in subcellular compartments.

questions.explanation: The primary cell wall of land plants is composed of polysaccharides, cellulose, hemicelluloses, and pectin.

questions.text: Which type of leucoplasts store lipids?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Amyloplasts store starch.
Leucoplasts are classified into three groups: amyloplasts, elaioplasts or osteoblasts, and proteinoplasts. Elaioplasts store lipids.
Proteinoplasts store proteins.
Etioplasts are photosynthetically inactive plastids that accumulate when light levels are too low for chloroplast maturation.

questions.explanation: Leucoplasts are classified into three groups: amyloplasts (which store starch), elaioplasts or osteoblasts (store lipids), and proteinoplasts (store proteins).

questions.text: Which type of movement through the cell membrane is not an energy-consuming process?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In the cytoplasm, macromolecules or large particles are moved across the plasma membrane by vesicles. Endocytosis and exocytosis are examples of vesicle transport. They are both active, energy-consuming processes.

In the cytoplasm, macromolecules or large particles are moved across the plasma membrane by vesicles. Endocytosis and exocytosis are examples of vesicle transport. They are both active, energy-consuming processes.
Active transport is the transport of molecules that require the use of energy, in the form of an energy-storing chemical called ATP (adenosine triphosphate) to help them cross the cell membrane.

Osmosis is a form of passive transport when water molecules move from low solute concentration (high water concentration) to high solute or low water concentration across a membrane that is not permeable to the solute.

questions.explanation: 'Osmosis' is the movement of a solvent across a semipermeable membrane, toward a higher concentration of solute (lower concentration of solvent). Both 'diffusion' and 'osmosis' are passive transport processes, which means they do not require any input of extra energy to occur.

questions.text: What is the role of cholesterol in the cell membrane?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: It functions in joining adjacent cells together.
options.explanation: Cells are connected by tight junctions and gap junctions. Desmosomes are a specialized type of intercellular junctions.

options.text: It functions in recognizing foreign cells.
options.explanation: T cells only recognize foreign antigens that are displayed on the surfaces of the body's own cells.

options.text: It increases the rate of transport of ions across the membrane.
options.explanation: Many stimuli increase the rate of ions flowing across or into the cell membrane. The main types of stimuli that are known to cause ion channels to open are a change in the voltage across the membrane (voltage-gated channels), a mechanical stress (mechanically gated channels), or the binding of a ligand (ligand-gated channels).
Cholesterol is a lipid molecule that is present in the lipid bilayer of the cell membrane.

questions.explanation: 'Cholesterol' is interspersed between the phospholipid bilayer. In case of temperature deviation, the fatty acid tails may melt or freeze. Changing the fluidity of the membrane, that is, with an increase in temperature, the phospholipid entities tend to move away from each other, and in low temperatures, the molecules will move very close to each other, disturbing the membrane's ability to transport substances. Cholesterol is the stabilizing molecule that prevents extreme movement of phospholipids during temperature fluctuation So, the correct answer is 'It stabilizes the cell membrane.'

questions.text: Fibers of the extracellular matrix are attached to _ in the plasma membrane.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: As given in the figure, it can clearly be seen that the fibers of the extracellular matrix only attach to the intrinsic proteins of the cell membrane
As given in the figure, it can clearly be seen that the fibers of the extracellular matrix only attach to the intrinsic proteins of the cell membrane
As given in the figure, it can clearly be seen that the fibers of the extracellular matrix only attach to the intrinsic proteins of the cell membrane
The fibers of the extracellular matrix are attached to the proteins involved in the plasma membrane

questions.explanation: A network of collagen fibers and proteoglycans is found outside of the cell. Collagen connects to integrin proteins in the plasma membrane via fibronectin. On the inside of the cell, the integrins link up to the microfilaments of the cytoskeleton.

questions.text: _ organelles are involved in the synthesis of the plant cell wall.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The ER is involved in cell to cell communication via the plasmodesmata, and, in specialized cells, it serves as a storage site for proteins. The plant ER is also equipped with enzymes and structural proteins which are involved in the process of oil body biogenesis and lipid storage.

In plant cells, the Golgi apparatus further serves as the site at which the complex polysaccharides of the cell wall are synthesized. Golgi Apparatus is the organelle that are involved in the synthesis of the plant cell wall.
Spherosomes (or Oleosomes) are single membrane-bound cell organelles that are found only in plant cells. It is also called plant lysosome.
In plant cells, peroxisomes play vital roles in metabolism, since they house many processes including fatty acid β-oxidation, glyoxylate cycles in seedlings, and photorespiration in leaves.

questions.explanation: The Golgi and plasma membranes are the two main sites, where constituents of the cell wall are synthesized.
hints: synthesis of the plant cell wall.

questions.text: Select the pair of organs, that contains a large number of mitochondria:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The liver contains a large number of mitochondria but the stomach doesn't have as many mitochondria as the liver.
Muscles contain a large number of mitochondria but the stomach doesn't have as many mitochondria as muscles.

The liver contains a large number of mitochondria but the heart doesn't have as many mitochondria as the liver. Liver cells contain 1000-2000 mitochondria in each cell. Heart contains about 5,000 mitochondria per cell.

Tissues and organs, that need a lot of energy, have large numbers of mitochondria in their cells. For example, liver cells and muscle cells contain a lot of mitochondria. Muscle cells are associated with a large number of mitochondria as they require more ATP (energy) to function than other cells.

questions.explanation: Tissues and organs, that need a lot of energy, have large numbers of mitochondria in their cells. For example, liver cells and muscle cells contain a lot of mitochondria. Muscle cells are associated with a large number of mitochondria as they require more ATP (energy) to function than other cells.

questions.text: Which of the following cells does not have a nucleus?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Muscle cells have a nucleus per cell. Different types of muscle cells have different numbers of nuclei. For example: Skeletal muscle cells are long, cylindrical, and striated. They are multinucleated meaning that they have more than one nucleus.

The Nucleus of a neuron is an oval-shaped membrane-bound structure found in the soma or body of the neuron. It contains the nucleolus and chromosomes, necessary for the coded production of proteins within the cell. The nucleolus of the nucleus produces ribosomes.
White blood cells, on the other hand, are actively working to help protect the body from infections and they need to be able to make proteins to do this and therefore must keep their nuclei.

Unlike most other eukaryotic cells, mature red blood cells don't have nuclei. When they enter the bloodstream for the first time, they eject their nuclei and organelles so they can carry more hemoglobin, and thus, more oxygen. Mature red blood cells do not need to have a nucleus because they have already made all the proteins they will ever need.

questions.explanation: Unlike most other eukaryotic cells, mature red blood cells don't have nuclei. When they enter the bloodstream for the first time, they eject their nuclei and organelles so, they can carry more hemoglobin, and thus, more oxygen. Each red blood cell has a lifespan of around 100–120 days.

questions.text: The most abundant organic compounds in a mammalian cell are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Proteins make up 18% of the cells composition in mammals. You might choose water, as it makes 70% of the cell. However, water is not an organic compound.
Proteins make up 18% of the cells composition in mammals. Lipids are only 3% in mammalian cell.
Proteins make up 18% of the cells composition in mammals. Carbodydrates are only 4% in mammalian cell.
Proteins make up 18% of the cells composition in mammals. You might choose water, as it makes 70% of the cell. However, water is not an organic compound.

questions.explanation: Proteins make up 18% of the cells composition in mammals. You might choose water, as it makes 70% of the cell. However, water is not an organic compound.

questions.text: The second most abundant bio element in the human body is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Oxygen has the highest percentage composition in the human body i.e., 65%.
Carbon has the 2nd highest percentage composition in the human body i.e., 18%.
Hydrogen has the 3rd highest percentage composition in the human body i.e., 10%.
Nitrogen has the 4th highest percentage composition in the human body i.e., 3%.

questions.explanation: Carbon is the second most abundant element in the human body, accounting for 18% of body weight. Oxygen is the most abundant, constituting about 61- 65% of the mass of the human body.

questions.text: Lecithin is formed by combining phosphatidic acid with _:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Phosphoserine is an ester of serine and phosphoric acid. Phosphoserine is a component of many proteins as the result of posttranslational modifications.

Lecithin, also called Phosphatidylcholine, is a group of phospholipids (phosphoglycerides) that are important in cell structure and metabolism. Lecithins are composed of phosphoric acid, choline, esters of glycerol, and two fatty acids; the chain length, position, and degree of unsaturation of these fatty acids vary, and this variation results in different lecithins with different biological functions.

When inositol and phosphatidic acid combine, they form Inositol hexa phosphoric acid (IP6) or Phytic acid, a natural antioxidant of some leguminous plants, known to act as a protective agent for seed storage in plants.
O-Phosphoethanolamine, also known as calamine phosphoric acid or ethanolamine O-phosphate, is formed by the combination of phosphatidic acid with ethanolamine.

questions.explanation: 'Lecithin', also called Phosphatidylcholine, is a group of phospholipids (phosphoglycerides) that are important in cell structure and metabolism. Lecithins are composed of phosphoric acid, cholines, esters of glycerol, and two fatty acids; the chain length, position, and degree of unsaturation of these fatty acids vary, and this variation results in different lecithins with different biological functions.

questions.text: NAD is an example of _:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mononucleotide means a substance having one nucleotide in its structure. However, NAD only has two nucleotides in its structure.

A dinucleotide means a substance having two nucleotides in its structure. Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NAD) is a cofactor central to metabolism. Found in all living cells, NAD is called a dinucleotide because it consists of two nucleotides, joined through their phosphate groups.
A trinucleotide means a substance that has three nucleotides in its structure. However, NAD only has two nucleotides in its structure.
Tetranucleotide means a substance that has four nucleotides in its structure. However, NAD only has two nucleotides in its structure.

questions.explanation: Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NAD) is a cofactor central to metabolism. Found in all living cells, NAD is called a dinucleotide because it consists of two nucleotides, joined through their phosphate groups. questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Each amino acid is coded by 3 nucleotides. To form a protein molecule of 142 amino acids, we multiply 142 with 3. The product is 426 . None of these options are correct.
Each amino acid is coded by 3 nucleotides. To form a protein molecule of 142 amino acids , we multiply 142 with 3. The product is 426 . None of these options are correct
Each amino acid is coded by 3 nucleotides, hence to form a protein molecule of 142 amino acids, 142 is multiplied by 3 nucleotides to answer 426 nucleotides. This option is correct.
Each amino acid is coded by 3 nucleotides. To form a protein molecule of 142 amino acids, we multiply 142 with 3. The product is 426 . None of these options are correct.

questions.explanation: Each amino acid is coded by 3 nucleotides, hence to form a protein molecule of 142 amino acids, 142 multiplied by 3 nucleotides would be needed, which comes out to be 426.

questions.text: The basic structural framework of all types of membranes are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Phospholipids are major membrane lipids that consist of lipid bilayers. This basic cellular structure acts as a barrier to protect the cell against various environmental insults and more importantly, enables multiple cellular processes to occur in subcellular compartments.

Glycoproteins act as cell surface markers. Glycoproteins perform vital biochemical and structural functions. They enable cells to recognize another cell as familiar or foreign, which is called cell-cell recognition. They also help cells attach to and bind to other cells, which is called cell adhesion.

Lipoproteins are formed by the combination of lipids and proteins and serve as the basic structural framework of all types of membranes in the cells. Lipoproteins help maintain the lipid composition of cell membranes.
Nucleoproteins are important for the structure and function of the cell nucleus. They are responsible for the transport of genetic material within the nucleus.

questions.explanation: Lipoproteins formed by the combination of lipids and proteins serve as the basic structural framework of all types of membranes in the cells.

questions.text: Non-protein and organic, detachable co-factors are called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: An activator, in the context of enzymes, is a substance or molecule that enhances the activity of an enzyme. Activators can work by various mechanisms, such as promoting enzyme-substrate binding, stabilizing the enzyme's active conformation, or facilitating other steps in the enzymatic reaction.

A prosthetic group is a non-protein molecule that is tightly and permanently attached to a protein, usually an enzyme, to help the protein's function. Unlike cofactors or coenzymes, which are temporarily bound to enzymes during catalysis, prosthetic groups remain attached to the protein as a permanent part of its structure.

A coenzyme is a non-protein organic compound that binds with an enzyme to catalyze a reaction. Coenzymes are essential for the proper functioning of many enzymes, and they often act as carriers of specific functional groups or electrons during metabolic reactions. Unlike enzymes, coenzymes are not proteins but are small organic molecules, and they are sometimes derived from vitamins.

An apoenzyme, also known as an apoprotein, is the protein component of an enzyme that is inactive by itself. To become an active enzyme, the apoenzyme needs to bind with a non-protein component called a cofactor or a coenzyme. The complete, active enzyme is then referred to as a holoenzyme.

questions.explanation: A coenzyme is a non-protein organic compound that binds with an enzyme to catalyze a reaction. Coenzymes are essential for the proper functioning of many enzymes, and they often act as carriers of specific functional groups or electrons during metabolic reactions. Unlike enzymes, coenzymes are not proteins but are small organic molecules, and they are sometimes derived from vitamins.

questions.text: When an inhibitor binds to an enzyme other than the active site and alters its structure, then it is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Competitive inhibitors are a type of enzyme inhibitor that competes with the substrate for binding to the active site of an enzyme. These inhibitors resemble the structure of the substrate and can bind to the enzyme, but they do not undergo the same chemical reactions as the substrate.

A non-competitive inhibitor is a type of enzyme inhibitor that binds to an enzyme at a location other than the active site. This binding causes a conformational change in the enzyme, reducing its catalytic activity. Non-competitive inhibitors bind to a site on the enzyme that is distinct from the active site. This binding can occur whether or not the substrate is already bound to the enzyme. An example of a non-competitive inhibitor is heavy metal ions that can bind to enzymes, causing a decrease in catalytic activity.
A reversible inhibitor form weak linkages with the enzyme. Their efect can be neutralized completely or partly by an increase in the concentration of the substrate.

An irreversible inhibitor check the reaction rate by occupying the active sites or destroying the globular structure. They occupy the active sites by forming covalent bonds or they may physically block the active sites.

questions.explanation: A non-competitive inhibitor is a type of enzyme inhibitor that binds to an enzyme at a location other than the active site. This binding causes a conformational change in the enzyme, reducing its catalytic activity. Non-competitive inhibitors bind to a site on the enzyme that is distinct from the active site. This binding can occur whether or not the substrate is already bound to the enzyme. An example of a non-competitive inhibitor is heavy metal ions that can bind to enzymes, causing a decrease in catalytic activity.

questions.text: Cyanides are potent poisons of the living organism and can kill by inhibiting _, essential for cellular respiration:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cyanides are potent poisons for living organisms because they can kill a living organism by inhibiting cytochrome oxidase essential for cellular respiration. They block the actions of these enzymes by combining with iron which may be present in the prosthetic group.
Dehydrogenases are enzymes that catalyze reduction reactions through the transfer of hydrogen ions (protons) from the substrate to an acceptor or coenzyme.
Hydrolase is a class of hydrolytic enzymes that are commonly used as biochemical catalysts which utilize water as a hydroxyl group donor during the substrate breakdown.

Nuclease refers to any enzyme that cleaves nucleic acids. Nucleases, which belong to the class of enzymes called hydrolases, are usually specific in action, ribonucleases acting only upon ribonucleic acids (RNA) and deoxyribonucleases acting only upon deoxyribonucleic acids (DNA)

questions.explanation: Cyanide poisons the mitochondrial electron transport chain within cells and renders the body unable to derive energy (adenosine triphosphate-ATP) from oxygen. Specifically, it binds to the a3 portion (complex IV) of cytochrome oxidase and prevents cells from using oxygen, causing rapid death.

questions.text: During feedback inhibition, which of the following structural part of enzyme is involved?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This term refers to the same region on the enzyme where the substrate binds and catalysis occurs
This term refers to the same region on the enzyme where the substrate binds and catalysis occurs
This term refers to the same region on the enzyme where the substrate binds and catalysis occurs

Feedback inhibition is a regulatory mechanism in which the end product of a metabolic pathway inhibits an earlier enzyme in the pathway. This inhibition occurs when the end product binds to an allosteric site on the enzyme

questions.explanation: Feedback inhibition is usually accomplished through something called an “allosteric site” – a site on an enzyme, that changes the shape of an enzyme, and subsequently, the behavior of the active site.

questions.text: _ is involved in lipid synthesis/metabolism.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The smooth endoplasmic reticulum also synthesizes lipids, especially phospholipids, and cholesterol. These lipids are rapidly incorporated into the lipid bilayer of the endoplasmic reticulum, thus causing the endoplasmic reticulum to grow more extensively. Other functions of the SER include synthesis of carbohydrates, lipids, and steroid hormones; detoxification of medications and poisons; and storage of calcium ions.

RER is associated with many roles in protein synthesis, which also include post-translational modifications, folding, and sorting. Membrane-bound ribosomes in the RER translate the mature mRNA transcript into amino acids that are attached to become polypeptides.

Mitochondria are membrane-bound cell organelles that generate most of the chemical energy needed to power the cell's biochemical reactions. Chemical energy produced by the mitochondria is stored in a small molecule called ATP.
The vacuoles are essential cytoplasmic organs (organelles), performing functions such as storage, ingestion, digestion, excretion, and expulsion of excess water.

questions.explanation: 'Smooth ER' is involved in lipid synthesis and metabolism. 'Rough ER' is involved in protein synthesis. The main job of 'mitochondria' is to perform cellular respiration. This means it takes in nutrients from the cell, breaks them down, and turns them into energy. 'Vacuoles', on the other hand, are organelles existing mainly to isolate potentially harmful waste materials. They do their task effectively by simply engulfing the waste material.

questions.text: If another molecule, having a shape very similar to the enzymes substrate, binds to its active site, it would then _ the enzyme’s function.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Inhibitors tend to inhibit the enzyme's activity, not fasten them.

If another molecule, having a shape very similar to the enzyme's substrate, binds to its active site, it would then inhibit the enzyme's function. This type of inhibition is known as competitive inhibition. The molecule that is similar in shape to the substrate is called the competitive inhibitor. By binding to the active site, the competitive inhibitor prevents the substrate from binding to the enzyme and, consequently, inhibits the catalytic activity of the enzyme.

This type of inhibition is known as competitive inhibition. The molecule that is similar in shape to the substrate is called the competitive inhibitor. By binding to the active site, the competitive inhibitor prevents the substrate from binding to the enzyme and, consequently, inhibits the catalytic activity of the enzyme.

This type of inhibition is known as competitive inhibition. The molecule that is similar in shape to the substrate is called the competitive inhibitor. By binding to the active site, the competitive inhibitor prevents the substrate from binding to the enzyme and, consequently, inhibits the catalytic activity of the enzyme.

questions.text: Myofibrils, within the muscle fibers, contain thick and thin filaments made up of _ and _ respectively:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Myosin is a thick filament that is present in the A band of myofibrils, whereas Actin is a thin filament that is present in the I band of myofibril respectively.
Globulin and Albumin are simple proteins, they are neither thick nor thin filaments of myofibril.
Both tropomyosin and troponin are thin filaments (in addition to actin) in the I band of myofibril.
Fibrinogen is a complex glycoprotein present in high concentrations in plasma. Fibrinogen is converted to fibrin, which stabilizes blood clots and promotes hemostasis.

questions.explanation: The myofibrils are made up of thick and thin myofilaments, which help give the muscle its striated appearance. The thick filaments are composed of 'myosin', and the thin filaments are predominantly 'actin'.

questions.text: The correct option about spinal nerves is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Spinal nerves have a total of 62 nerves i.e., 31 pairs of nerves.
All the spinal nerves are mixed nerves.

options.text: The dorsal root contains sensory neurons
options.explanation: This option is correct. It is true that the dorsal root contains sensory neurons.

options.text: The ventral root contains motor neurons
options.explanation: This option is also correct. It is true that the ventral root contains motor neurons.
Since both option C and D are correct, Hence the Option E (all) is the most suitable option.

questions.explanation: As the dorsal root emerges from the intervertebral neural foramina, it forms the dorsal root ganglion (DRG). The DRG contains the cell body of the sensory neurons. These neurons relay sensory neural messages from the periphery to the central nervous system (brain and spinal cord). The ventral (anterior) or motor root consists of axons from the lower motor neurons in the ventral horn of the spinal cord. The ventral roots typically have fewer fibers than the dorsal roots, but they are of a larger caliber because they contain thick axons originating from the large lower motor neurons.

questions.text: _ is sometimes given by injection as an emergency treatment in cardiac arrest.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Acetylcholine is the neurotransmitter at neuromuscular junctions, at synapses in the ganglia of the visceral motor system. It is a major neurotransmitter outside the CNS i.e., in the PNS.

Dopamine is a chemical released in the brain that makes you feel good. Having the right amount of dopamine is important both for your body and your brain. Dopamine helps nerve cells to send messages to each other.

Serotonin is a chemical that carries messages between nerve cells in the brain and throughout your body. Serotonin plays a key role in such body functions as mood, sleep, digestion, nausea, wound healing, bone health and blood clotting.
Epinephrine is the primary drug administered during cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) to reverse cardiac arrest. Epinephrine increases arterial blood pressure and coronary perfusion during CPR.

questions.explanation: When CPR does not generate coronary perfusion of more than 15-20 mmHg, return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) rarely or never occurs. After more than a few minutes of cardiac arrest, arterial tone collapses and epinephrine or another vasoconstrictor is essential for restoration of cardiac activity.

questions.text: Which one of the following is a neurotransmitter?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Dopamine is a neurotransmitter made in your brain. It plays a role as a “reward center” and in many body functions, including memory, movement, motivation, mood, attention, etc.

Hydrochloric acid plays an important role in your digestion and immunity. It helps break down protein and absorb essential nutrients, and it helps control viruses and bacteria that might otherwise infect your stomach.
Sodium ion (Na+) is the principal extracellular cation and solute and is essential for generation of action potentials in nervous and cardiac tissue.
Calcium ions (Ca+) trigger contraction by reaction with regulatory proteins that in the absence of calcium prevent interaction of actin and myosin.

questions.explanation: 'Dopamine' is a neurotransmitter that helps send signals in the brain. Neurotransmitters are chemicals made by nerve cells called neurons. They're used to communicate messages across different parts of the brain and between the brain and the rest of the body. 'Dopamine' is involved mainly in controlling movement.

questions.text: Which hormone is chemically a steroid?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: ADH or Vasopressin is essentially a polypeptide hormone.
Thyroxine is essentially an amino acid derivative.
Cortisone is essentially a steroid hormone.
Insulin is essentially a protein hormone.

questions.explanation: Steroid hormones are fat-soluble molecules made from cholesterol. Among these are the three major sex hormone groups: estrogens, androgens, and progesterones. Males and females make all three, just in different amounts. The steroids that are made almost exclusively in the adrenal glands are cortisol, 11-deoxycortisol, aldosterone, corticosterone, and 11-deoxycorticosterone. Most other steroid hormones, including estrogens, are made by the adrenal glands and the gonads.

questions.text: Lymph nodes are not present:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Lymph nodes are small, bean-shaped organs that filter substances in your body. Cells that help fight infections make up your lymph nodes along with lymph tissue. There are hundreds of lymph nodes throughout your body. The most well-known places where you'll find lymph nodes are in your armpits, neck, and groin.

Lymph nodes are small, bean-shaped organs that filter substances in your body. Cells that help fight infections make up your lymph nodes along with lymph tissue. There are hundreds of lymph nodes throughout your body. The most well-known places where you'll find lymph nodes are in your armpits, neck, and groin.

Lymph nodes are small, bean-shaped organs that filter substances in your body. Cells that help fight infections make up your lymph nodes along with lymph tissue. There are hundreds of lymph nodes throughout your body. The most well-known places where you'll find lymph nodes are in your armpits, neck and groin.
The spleen is not a lymph node itself. It is am organ located in the upper left abdomen.

questions.explanation: Lymph nodes are present in neck region, axilla, and groin of humans. Lymph node is a mass of connective tissue. Several afferent lymph vessels enter lymph node which is emptied into single efferent lymph vessel. Spleen is a lymphoid mass.

questions.text: Which brain portion is responsible for controlling body coordination?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The medulla oblongata helps control vital processes like your heartbeat, breathing, and blood pressure.
The main job of the amygdala is to regulate emotions, such as fear and aggression. The amygdala is also involved in tying emotional meaning to our memories, reward processing, and decision-making.
The cerebellum is part of your brain that helps coordinate and regulate a wide range of functions and processes in both your brain and body. The cerebellum controls balance and movement and posture.
The pons is a part of your brainstem, a structure that links your brain to your spinal cord. It handles unconscious processes such as your sleep-wake cycle and breathing.

questions.explanation: The cerebellum, in the back of the brain, controls balance, coordination, and fine muscle control (e.g., walking). It also functions to maintain posture and equilibrium.

questions.text: The estrogen hormone secretion during the oogenesis is stimulated by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: When the level of estrogen is sufficiently high, it produces a sudden release of LH, usually around day thirteen of the cycle.

Inhibin is a protein secreted by the Sertoli cells in men and by the granulosa cells in women respectively. It inhibits the synthesis and release of the follicle-stimulating hormone in the pituitary gland and reduces the hypothalamic LH - releasing hormone content.

As the follicle grows, blood levels of estrogen rise significantly by cycle day seven. This increase in estrogen begins to inhibit the secretion of FSH. The fall in FSH allows smaller follicles to die off.

Testosterone is a male sex hormone, or androgen, produced in a woman's ovaries in small amounts. Combined with estrogen, the female sex hormone, testosterone helps with the growth, maintenance, and repair of a woman's reproductive tissues, bone mass, and human behaviors.

questions.explanation: Follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), the hormone responsible for starting follicle (egg) development, causes the level of estrogen, the primary female hormone, to rise.

questions.text: In human (males), the inhibin hormone is produced by:

A and C are incorrect because strong acid with its salt does not form a buffer solution; weak acids with its salt forms a buffer solution. D is theoretically not possible because a weak acid can not react with a weak base to form a salt.

questions.text: The pH of human blood is maintained between:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.explanation: Blood is normally slightly basic, with a normal pH range of about 7.35 to 7.45. Usually, the body maintains the pH of blood close to 7.40. This is because most enzymes present in the blood have their optimum pH in this range.

questions.text: In the reaction; H2 + CO2 <-> H2O + CO The decrease in the concentration of reactants and products cause the equilibrium to shift:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The reaction will move towards left if the concentration of reactants is decreased.
It will move towards right if concentration of product will decrease.
Since both sides concentration is decreasing, hence there will be no effect on concentration.

options.text: Equilibrium will shift towards both the directions
options.explanation: This is not possible that equilibrium shifts in both directions.

questions.explanation: The decrease in the concentration of either side of the reaction equation favors that very side of the equation. However, if both sides (the reactants and the products) show a decrease in concentration, this will render no change to the equilibrium.

questions.text: At equilibrium the concentration of reactants and product become:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Zero cannot be the correct option as if this happens then the reaction would not occur. If the concentration of reactant is zero resultantly no products will be formed. Similarly when both the reactants and product become zero then no reaction occurs as there would be nothing to react with.

At equilibrium, the concentration of reactants and products is not equal. If this occurs than equilibrium cannot be established. The rates of the forward and reverse reactions must be equal. The amount of reactants and products do not have to be equal. However, after equilibrium is attained, the amounts of reactants and products will be constant.

Chemical equilibrium is a state in which the rate of the forward reaction equals the rate of the backward reaction. In other words, there is no net change in concentrations of reactants and products. This kind of equilibrium is also called dynamic equilibrium. All reactions tend towards a state of chemical equilibrium, the point at which both the forward process and the reverse process are taking place at the same rate. Since the forward and reverse rates are equal, the concentrations of the reactants and products are constant at equilibrium.
At equilibrium, the concentration of reactants and products is not infinite. Rather they are constant.

questions.explanation: Equilibrium occurs when the rate of forward reaction becomes equal to the rate of backward reaction, thus making the concentration of reactants and products constant hence 'option C' is correct. 'Option A' is very wrong because concentrations of reactants and products do not become zero at equilibrium. It can be understood by common sense that if the concentrations were to become zero, then the reaction will not occur. 'Option B' is the most commonly chosen incorrect answer. Equilibrium is frequently misunderstood as equal concentrations of reactants and products, which is extremely wrong! 'Option D' is wrong because concentrations of reactants and products are limited (not infinite).

questions.text: The effect of temperature on the rate of a reaction is given by:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The Henderson-Hassel Balch equation provides a relationship between the pH of acids (in aqueous solutions) and their pKa (acid dissociation constant). The pH of a buffer solution can be estimated with the help of this equation when the concentration of the acid and its conjugate base, or the base and the corresponding conjugate acid, are known.

The ideal gas equation is formulated as PV = nRT. In this equation, P refers to the pressure of the ideal gas, V is the volume of the ideal gas, n is the total amount of ideal gas that is measured in terms of moles, R is the universal gas constant, and T is the temperature.

The Arrhenius equation describes the relationship between the rate of reaction and temperature for many physical and chemical reactions. The Arrhenius equation is the appropriate model for predicting parameters according to phase changes, and it can be used to estimate the properties of materials as temperature and energy change.

Van der Waals forces are weak electrostatic forces that attract neutral molecules to one another. Particles in liquid or air vibrate and move constantly. Thus, they collide with other particles, including the media's particles such as water molecules—the process known as Brownian motion.

questions.explanation: In physical chemistry, the 'Arrhenius equation' is a formula for the temperature dependence of reaction rates- k= Ae(-Ea/RT) where A= Arrhenius constant, Ea= activation energy, R= universal gas constant, T= absolute temperature. So, C stands correct. 'Henderson’s equation' is used to calculate the pH of the buffer solution, not the effect of temperature on reaction rates. The general gas equation and Van Der Waals equation are both used to study the changes in the volume of ideal gases at different pressures and temperatures. Both of these equations have nothing to do with reaction rates.

questions.text: The rate of reaction:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The rate of reaction does not increase as the reaction proceeds. This is because the concentration of reactants decreases progressively.

The reaction rate, in chemistry, is the speed at which a chemical reaction proceeds. It is often expressed in terms of either the concentration (amount per unit volume) of a product that is formed in a unit of time or the concentration of a reactant that is consumed in a unit of time. The rate of reaction decreases as the reaction proceeds because the concentration of reactants is high at the start and with time, this concentration decreases and the reaction gets slower. So, the rate of reaction is dependent on concentration and hence, decreases with the passage of time.

options.text: Remains the same as the reaction proceeds
options.explanation: The rate of reaction does not remain the same. With the passage of time the concentration of reactants decreases as they are converted to products. While the concentration of product increases as reactants are converted to the products. Hence, in no way the rate of reaction is constant.

options.text: May decrease or increase as the reaction proceeds
options.explanation: There is only one way and no other in these processes. It cannot be said that its may or may not. Hence, this option is also incorrect.

questions.explanation: The rate of reaction is dependent on the concentration of reactants. The rate of a reaction decreases as time progresses. As a reaction progresses, the reactants are being turned into products, decreasing the concentration of the reactants, consequently, lowering the rate of reaction.

questions.text: 0.5 molar solution NaOH contains:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Calcuation suggests D is correct
Calculation suggests D is correct
Calculation suggests D is correct

Atomic mass of Na: 23g/mol Atomic mass of O: 16g/mol Atomic mass of H: 1g/mol Molar mass of NaOH: 23+6+1= 40 g/mol Molarity= number of moles= 0.5 For a solution to be one molar, one mole of a substance has to be dissolved in 1dm3 of the solvent. To find out the mass of NaOH, we will use; No.of moles= mass/ molar mass 0.5= m/40 m= 40x0.5 m= 20g

questions.explanation: The atomic mass of Na is 23g/mol, the atomic mass of O is 16g/mol, and the atomic mass of H is 1g/mol so, by adding all three atomic masses, we can obtain the mass number of NaOH, which is 23+16+1=40g/mol. For a solution to be 1 molar, 1 mole of a substance has to be dissolved in 1 dm3 of the solvent. Since we have to find out the mass of NaOH needed to make a 0.5 molar solution, we will dissolve 0.5 moles of NaOH in 1 dm3 of the solvent. 0.5 mol of NaOH corresponds to 20g of NaOH which can be worked out using the proportion method that if 1 mol of NaOH contains 40g of NaOH (as calculated above) then 0.5 mol of NaOH will contain 20g of NaOH. If the proportion method seems difficult, the formula relating mass with moles can be used which is: n=m/M; where M is molar mass, n is the number of moles, and m is the mass of NaOH for n moles. (0.5) = m/(40) m=(40)(0.5)=20g.

questions.text: The breakdown of a substance with current is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The word ‘Thermo’ means ‘heat’ and ‘lysis’ means ‘breakdown’. Thus, thermolysis is the breakdown of a substance by the action of heat.

Catalysis is a term describing a process in which the rate and/or the outcome of the reaction is influenced by the presence of a substance (the catalyst) that is not consumed during the reaction and that is subsequently removed if it is not to constitute as an impurity in the final product.

The word ‘electro’ refers to ‘electricity’, while the word ‘lysis’ means ‘breakdown’. Electrolysis is a process by which an electric current is passed through a substance to effect a chemical change. A chemical change is one in which the substance loses or gains an electron (oxidation or reduction).
The word ‘photo’ refers to light and ‘lysis’ to ‘ breakdown’. Hence, photolysis is the breakdown in a process by the action of light.

questions.explanation: 'Electrolysis' is the breakdown of a substance with the use of electricity. 'Thermolysis' is the breakdown of a substance using heat energy. 'Photolysis' is the breakdown of a substance using light energy. 'Catalysis' is, however, a misnomer; it is not the term used for the breakdown of a substance rather it implies the speeding up of the reaction rate.

questions.text: The stronger the oxidizing agent, the greater is the:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The oxidation potential is the tendency of an electrode to lose electrons. While oxidizing agent is a substance in a redox chemical reaction that gains or "accepts"/"receives" an electron from a reducing agent. Hence, this option is incorrect.
Reduction potential is the tendency of an electrode to gain electrons. Hence, a strong oxidizing agent is the one that gains an electron as well. Thus, this is the appropriate answer.
Redox potential is ‘’oxidation-reduction potential’’. And this is not possible for a single agent to be redoxing agent. Hence, this option is also incorrect.
The oxidizing agent has no relationship with the EMF of a cell. Hence, if the oxidizing agent is stronger, the EMF will not be greater.

questions.explanation: The stronger the oxidizing agent, the greater will be its reduction potential. Oxidizing ability is the ability of a substance to oxidize other substances. To oxidize other substances, it has to reduce itself so, to be a strong oxidizing agent one must have great reduction potential. An example is Fluorine which is one of the best oxidizing agents and has a reduction potential of +2.87V.

questions.text: Which of the following Halogens molecules has maximum bond energy?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: We would expect the B.E of F-F to be the highest but this isn’t so. The reason is that the fluorine atom having the highest electronegativity is smaller in size than the other atoms of its group. Due to this the inter-nuclear repulsive forces operating are larger than others and with a small amount of energy, the bond can be broken down.

Bond energy( B.E ) is a measure of the bond strength of a chemical bond, and is the amount of energy needed to break the atoms involved in a molecular bond into free atoms. B.E. depends on the atomic radius. Going down the group, in halogens, the bond energy decreases. It is because down the group the distance between the outermost electrons and nucleus decreases, decreasing the force of attraction between them. There is an exception in the halogen group which is of fluorine. This is because the atomic radius of fluorine atoms is so small that when two fluorine atoms form an F-F bond, the internuclear repulsive forces between them reduces the bond strength and thus the bond energy. That is why despite being on the top-most position, fluorine has lower bond energy. Where Chlorine is having the least radius thus, its B.E is the highest among all the members of its group.
Bromine’s radius is larger than Cl. As a result, its B.E. is also smaller than Cl. And this option is incorrect.
Iodine’s radius is even larger than bromine, thus having an even smaller B.E than bromine and being the incorrect option.

questions.explanation: Bond energy( B.E ) is a measure of the bond strength of a chemical bond, and is the amount of energy needed to break the atoms involved in a molecular bond into free atoms. B.E. depends on the atomic radius. Going down the group, in halogens, the bond energy decreases. It is because down the group the distance between the outermost electrons and nucleus decreases, decreasing the force of attraction between them. There is an exception in the halogen group which is of fluorine. This is because the atomic radius of fluorine atoms is so small that when two fluorine atoms form an F-F bond, the internuclear repulsive forces between them reduces the bond strength and thus the bond energy. That is why despite being on the top-most position, fluorine has lower bond energy. Where Chlorine is having the least radius thus, its B.E is the highest among all the members of its group.

options.explanation: According to Dalton's law of partial pressures, the total pressure by a mixture of gases is equal to the sum of the partial pressures of each of the constituent gases. Where P total = P1+ P2+ P3+…..Pn.
Avogadro's Law is stated mathematically as follows: Vn=k, where V is the volume of the gas, n is the number of moles of the gas, and k is a proportionality constant.

questions.text: Upon which factor is vapor pressure not dependent:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: The greater the temperature, the more molecules will gain sufficient K.E and hence, the greater the vapor pressure. Thus, the V.P of a liquid is dependent on the temperature.

The stronger the intermolecular forces, the lesser will be the V.P as the molecules will be tightly bound to each other and won’t be able to escape easily. Hence, the V.P is also inversely dependent on the intermolecular forces.
As the density increases the pressure also increases due to a decrease in the volume of the liquid. Hence, V.P is also dependent on the density of the liquid.

The V.P. of a liquid is independent of the surface area of the liquid. This is because the rate of evaporation is balanced with the rate of condensation. And any increase in the surface area will not be sufficient to increase the V.P until the molecules don’t have sufficient energy of action. Hence, this is the correct option.

questions.explanation: The surface area of liquid or solid in contact does not affect vapor pressure. Other factors do have an impact on vapor pressure. Higher density decreases vapor pressure, higher temperature increases vapor pressure and higher intermolecular forces also decrease the vapor pressure.

questions.text: If we take some water at 0oC and start heating it, we find that in the temperature range of 0oC to 4oC:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: At zero degrees the water is not in the liquid form but rather in the solid form i.e. ice. Hence, when at this state we increase the temperature from 0 to 4, the water contracts but not expand.

This anomalous behavior of water is due to the unusual structure of ice. Ice having hexagonal structure consists of spaces in between. When ice melts these spaces are filled by the other nearby molecules causing its volume to increase and density to decrease. Hence, water contracts on heating from 0 to 4 C.
Water cannot contract and expand simultaneously. Hence, this option is incorrect.
Since Option B is correct hence option D is also incorrect.

questions.explanation: If we take some water at 0 C and start heating it, we find that in the temperature range 0oC to 4oC, it contracts instead of expanding.on heating it further above 4 C, it expands.

questions.text: Molar heat of vaporization is the amount of heat required to convert one mole of:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: A liquid into its vapours at its boiling point
options.explanation: The heat of vaporization is defined as the amount of heat needed to turn 1g of a liquid into a vapor, without a rise in the temperature of the liquid i.e. at a constant temperature which is the boiling point. At B.P the temperature of a liquid remains the same. Hence, this is the correct option.
This option is also correct but here the limitation of temperature cannot be applied. Hence, we may consider it as incorrect.
This option is incorrect as the conversion of solid into vapors (i.e. liquid) is not vaporization. Rather it is called melting.

options.text: Solid into liquid at its melting point
options.explanation: Solid to liquid at its melting point is called melting not vaporization. Hence, this option is also incorrect.

questions.explanation: Molar heat of vapourization refers to the conversion of one mole of a liquid to its vapors at a constant temperature, which is the boiling point of the liquid. That is why option A is correct. Option B is partially correct because vaporization refers to the conversion of a liquid to gas, however, at a constant temperature. Options C and D are incorrect because vaporization does not involve the conversion of solids to liquids.

questions.text: At the transition temperature of a crystalline solid, the substance exists:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: At the transition temperature of a crystalline solid, the substance is not in the most stable geometric form. Rather it is in transition i.e. in between two stable forms. Hence, this option is incorrect.
The transition temperature only exists between two crystalline solid states and not liquid states.

options.text: In dynamic equilibrium between two crystalline forms
options.explanation: The transition temperature is the temperature at which a material changes from one crystal state (allotrope) to another. More formally, it is the temperature at which two crystalline forms of a substance can co-exist in equilibrium. Hence, this option is correct.

options.text: In one solid geometrical form only
options.explanation: The word transition shows that it is something in between, which can only exist in two. Hence, it does not apply to a single solid geometric form.

questions.explanation: Option C is correct because, from the thermodynamic standpoint, the transition temperature can be characterized by the temperature, T0, at which two crystalline phases can coexist.

questions.text: In a reversible reaction, catalyst lowers the activation energy of the:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: In a reversible reaction, the catalyst will not only lower the energy of the forward reaction only. This is because a catalyst speeds up the forward and back reaction to the same extent.
A catalyst is a substance that increases the rate of a reaction without being altered or used up in the reaction. Both the forward and reverse rates of the reaction are accelerated by a catalyst.

options.text: Forward as well as reverse reaction
options.explanation: The rate of a reaction can be increased by adding a suitable catalyst. A catalyst is a substance which increases the rate of a chemical reaction but it is not used up (remains chemically unchanged at the end). It provides an alternative reaction pathway of lower activation energy. In equilibrium reactions, whenever a catalyst is added it will effect both the forward and reverse reactions equally and thus allow the system to reach equilibrium faster.

options.text: Forward reaction but increases that of the reverse reaction
options.explanation: As mentioned in the above statements, a catalyst cannot affect one part of the reaction without having any effect on the other side of the reaction. Thus, this option is incorrect as catalyst cannot decrease the rate of forward reaction increasing the rate of reverse reaction.

questions.explanation: In the presence of a catalyst, both the forward and reverse reaction activation energy will decrease equally, thereby allowing the system to reach equilibrium faster. However, it is very important to keep in mind that the addition of a catalyst has no effect whatsoever on the final equilibrium position of the reaction.

questions.text: In motion of satellites, necessary centripetal force is provided by:

questions.text: When the antinodes, in stationary waves, are passing through their equilibrium position, the energy is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: As an object undergoes SHM, such as a mass-spring system or a simple pendulum, its kinetic energy and potential energy continuously transform from one form to the other. At the equilibrium position, where the displacement is zero, the kinetic energy is maximized, as the object reaches its maximum velocity. Simultaneously, the potential energy is at its minimum, as the displacement from the equilibrium position is zero. As the object moves away from the equilibrium, the kinetic energy decreases, reaching zero at the extremum positions, where the potential energy is maximized. The potential energy stored in the system is then converted back into kinetic energy as the object returns towards the equilibrium position

Stationary waves do not cause any energy transfer but the particles present contain energy. Particles at antinode have maximum velocity and hence maximum kinetic energy while crossing the equilibrium position, while potential energy is zero so the energy is wholly kinetic. When the antinodes are all at their extreme displacements, the energy stored is wholly potential.
